speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4 of the interview with Marion Horn. I'm Rivka Wakslak. It's September 13, 1995. You were telling me of how you went into the Italian commandant's office.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, by chance, noticed as we were walking along the railroad station that there was an office of the Italian commandant. And I suppose, having had the experience in Capljina with the Italians, we knew instinctively that if we went to the Germans we would have been taken out and shot immediately. There was-- they-- they would not have bothered taking us to any other concentration camp. They would just eliminated us immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we really had very little to lose. And we gambled. It's as simple as that. And I think it was us children who gambled with our lives and our parents'.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when our parents saw that we were making a very sharp turn into that office, they followed us. We asked if we could speak to the commandant. And somebody was a, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, attending there. Officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, yes, he's in the other office in the back. Would you like to speak to him? And we said, yes, we very much would like to speak with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came out and we told him. In the meantime, the Gestapo guy is in the office with us. And he said, these people have no business speaking with you. They're with me. And we said, no, we would like to speak with you. And he pushed the guy on the side and he said, come into my office. He closed the door behind. And we told him the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, look, this is the situation. He said, where are you people headed for? We said, we'd like to make it into Ljubljana, which is occupied by your people, by the Italians, and eventually to get into Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we told him we had false papers. We laid our cards on the table. We told him, look, this is the situation. We're escaping from. He said, OK, don't worry. I'll take care of you. He went outside and told the Gestapo, uh, these people are under my jurisdiction and I will take care of them. And they have my permission to be in Zagreb under my protection. The German was screaming, howling, carrying on, I will get you, I. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave us papers. The Italian commandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. But my father did. [INAUDIBLE] But I don't. There were certain things that I have complete recall. But there are certain things that-- I was still a child. And it was just overwhelming and overpowering. And this experience at that train station, um, I would like to insert here that about 20 years later, I went back to Yugoslavia. And I retraced-- as much as I could-- my steps of where I had been. And Marty knows that when I went to the railroad station in Zagreb, which was unchanged, I fell apart. I just literally let it all out. Uh. It was an unbelievably traumatic and emotional experience. Because it began and it was-- what could have happened there would've been much worse even than to have the machine guns pointed. Because it was-- it was a horror. And filled with impact, because it wasn't just us, it was the responsibility of our parents. They would have been-- we-- I felt was-- would have been on my conscience, really. So it was-- it was really, really tough. Um. When we came out of the station there, this guy was waiting in one of the corners. And he said, I'll get you people. If it's the last thing I do, I will get you. But he didn't. Uh. From there, we made arrangements-- excuse me-- to go to Ljubljana. And we had friends. And my father had left a stamp collection with some friends. And they came to meet us, and they handed my father the albums of stamps. Then we took the train-- with the false papers, of course-- from Zagreb in the evening to Ljubljana. And it was almost like the country divided. Because at a certain point, it's Slovenia. The Slovenian border became Italy at that point. And I luxuriated in on the train, and I lied down, and I went to sleep. And I used the albums of the stamps as a cushion. And then I put whatever blanket on top of that and I went to sleep. And we arrived in Ljubljana and we were met by friends. And I mentioned my father had friends everywhere. And he corresponded with people all over the world forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing that these people asked us was, uh, Solo, whatever happened to your stamp collection? And obviously it was a very valuable stamp collection with a whole collection of Austria, which was very valuable. And my father said, well, we have-- we have it with us. And they said, how did you get it across the border? And I said, oh, I used it as a pillow and I slept on it. And they said, did you realize there was a death penalty on this if you were caught? Even by the Italians, you would've been shot immediately. Transporting a valuable stamp collection across the border, there was some kind of a death penalty on it. So there are times when ignorance is bliss. And nobody was nervous because nobody knew. And I slept on it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ljubljana was another game. That was totally different. There we-- [SIGHS] my mother must have sold some more jewels. And we lived in an apartment building and shared an apartment with some Slovenian people who sublet a room, kitchen facilities, something like that. And the communists were the big problem there for the Italians. They were fighting the Italians. And they used to kidnap officers, soldiers, what have you, and kill them. And the Italians retaliated by making house searches from top to bottom. And when I say a house search, they would arrive at a house, at a building, they would surround the corner of the whole block, and then go from room to room, smash pillars, walls, to feel if there were any arms, any literature-- communist literature-- anybody hidden, and search for whoever their enemy was at that time. And they took my father and brother away as hostages 19 times. And what they did was they took-- they surren-- they rounded up hostages. And for any number-- let's say an officer was 25 hostages that was shot. They had numbers, they had value numbers on whether it was a-- a private, or it was a soldier, or it was. And the higher the rank, the higher the number of people. So. And they stamped it into the papers. They had a paper and they stamped each time they took you away. And they were gone 19 times. And 19 times we didn't know if we were ever going to see them again. And that was horrible. It was just-- I mean to see my father being dragged away, it was just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was different, but it has-- had its own horrors. And you never knew. Um.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I can't tell exactly how long we were there. But it was several months. And then from there, we requested to have permission to go to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did you request it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Italian government, um, that we would have-- be interned. We were prisoners, in essence. But we would like to be imprisoned in Italy. Uh. But it wasn't prisoners in the sense of being in a jail or in a concentration camp. You were interned in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were sent. We [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] received the papers and we went to a small town-- we were-- we didn't request. This was assigned to us. A small town in the province of Emilia, northern Italy-- to a small village between Bologna and Modena, little town by name of Castelfranco.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we had to report every day to the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was? What time of year? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I would say it was probably in 1942, towards the middle of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still so early in the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. It was the height of Mussolini and the height-- height of fascism.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Castelfranco?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably also around six months. Um.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We're not allowed to go to school, not allowed to go to any public places, not allowed to go in any movie house. Um. We were restricted. I made lots of friends there. I became very friendly with an elderly lady who had a grocery store and two daughters who took care of her grocery store. And I watched her cook. And I was fascinated. And she was just the most wonderful lady. I was allowed to ride a bicycle, and I used to run her numbers games for her. [LAUGHS] I was her bookie for her. And I-- and her daughters were not supposed to know that I'm running the numbers for her, their mother. And I used to watch her make bread and make the most wonderful sauces. And it was just-- I was in heaven. My brother became very friendly with a couple of sons from a baker. And they were wonderful people. Eventually, we didn't have to report to the police department every day, but once or twice a week. And it was fine if my father went. And they knew we were not running anywhere. Um. One day, the baker came to us and he said, listen, we just listened to Radio London. And there was a death penalty for listening to Radio London during the war. And we heard that Hitler is coming and taking over Italy. Um, I think it's going to become very dangerous for you people, and we want to help. You have to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, [SIGHS] again. You know. I had no roots for years. I mean, it was-- this was the first time that I really felt again that I was with human beings. And I had made friends. And it was very hard to leave these people. Um.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest son was a very big wheel in the Fascist Party. And he helped us from there to get to Milano. From, Milano we went to Como. And from there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the balance of my mother's rocks went towards smugglers, who took us to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were without shoes. Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it might make noise. We walked over the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Carried nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had some ruck-- knapsacks. Um. There were some change of underwear in the knapsacks. Um. Some really essential, you know, things. Uh. Very-- I had hardly anything at that point left at all. Um. As a matter of fact, I'm not sure whether that Raggedy Ann doll was still there or not. I don't think so. I think she was gone. Um. I had a change of underwear. I know I had another blouse. I had some socks. Uh. They took burlap and tied it around our feet. And we walked about eleven hours over the mountains. And I know that every time we got to a mountaintop, I said, is this it, and they said no. And then right behind the mountain top, there was another mountain top, and there was another one. And we heard dogs barking, and we knew that the Germans were parole-- patrolling this whole area, that they were on our scent, so to speak. Because a lot of people were escaping into Switzerland from every border in Switzerland. Um. We finally made it into the southern part of Switzerland, because we came, you know, from that area, uh, on a most gorgeous Sunday morning in April that I do remember because it wasn't far from my birthday. And we arrived to a mountain patrol station in Switzerland. And there were about three Swiss army men working in that station. And we went in. We said we escaping from Italy. We feel the Germans are on our trail. And they said-- the man in charge there said, sorry, you're going back. We can't take you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father said, I have lived in Switzerland for many, many years. I had a business in Switzerland. Would you let me make a phone call to Bern, which is the capital? Uh. He said-- the man said, well, I have no proof that you're telling the truth. And he said, well, I'm telling you. Would you let me make a phone call and I can substantiate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he said. No, no. No, no. Not making any phone calls. I don't care. You're Jews. I don't care who you are, what you are. Um. Go back. One of the other men in the station said to my father, you can use my phone. And the commandant up there said, you will pay for this dearly. And he said, I don't care. He said we-- we're humanitarian people. And if these people tell you-- and look at them. They don't even have shoes on. Um. I'm going to let them make a phone call. And then you can report me, and you can court-martial me, and do whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father kept in touch with him. As a matter of fact, it was very interesting. Because the partisans, the Italian partisans, were coming-- as they were fighting the Germans, they were coming into Switzerland. And then they were imprisoned in Switzerland. And a couple of guys that I-- my brother knew, one of them, the-- this man refused to let his brother in. And his brother was shot by the Germans at the border. And when he went back to Italy, on his way back, he stopped by and he killed this guy. He took a-- A iron chair and he smashed his skull in. So I'm not saying that it's right, but-- um.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father made that phone call?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father made the phone call. We were allowed in the country. My mother went straight to the hospital. She had developed terrible phlebitis. And she was hospitalized for almost three months. My brother was sent to a work camp with young people, which he didn't mind. My father was interned, because, you see, there was no computers in those days. They-- everything took time. And I was sent to a children's home. And I was the only girl. There were five boys there about my age, a little older. I don't know why they were there, but I don't remember that. That-- those details, I don't remember. There were younger children there. I remember that one of the boys was a German boy. He tried to rape me one night. And I knew he was going to try. And I was ready for him. And I hit him so hard that I don't think he ever tried it again with anybody. [LAUGHS] So. He must've been about 13 or 14 years old. And I think he got his lesson. Um. Then after the children's home that I was at, I started going to school. And that-- that-- that was interesting. Um. I kept going to different grades in school. A German-- a Jewish family wanted to take me in. And they were very nice. They showed me off to all their friends for the first week. And then they starved me, literally. Once I was no longer the object of showing off, they starved me, literally. They-- a slice of bread here and there, some old, sour soup that was left over from God knows when. And I ran away. And then eventually, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The family.

speaker: interviewee
text: --family got back together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did-- and you stayed in Lugano until the end of the war? This was Lugano?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, I stayed in Lugano until 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the war was over? Your family was together? Your father was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard the news at that point, you know. There-- there, you heard the news. But I also find out in retrospect that five days after we were evacuated from-- or taken away from-- Sarajevo that all the people in that camp were killed systematically. They were really wiped out. So we were very, very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your group-- did you find out what became of your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um. Most of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though they had been under Italian care?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 'Germans came back and the Ustase came back. And they did their cleansing down there, even then. And now that we have retro-- cleansing.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, you continued to live in Lugano for a number of years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally my father got the affidavit to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In November of 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we were on the ship on Thanksgiving. So I know it was November. I'm very bad with chronology, you know. I try. I've gotten better over the years now. But this is a-- I-- I-- I think so much of it has to do with the fact there was so much emotion, and so much chaos, and-- and-- such a upheaval in-- in-- in-- in life of a young person that, you know, we. We live here and we're afraid to change schools for children. And when I think in terms of, you know, how resilient a child is, but yet, you know. It's a little different changing schools than having your whole total world collapsing around you. It's-- it's quite a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet you did very well academically.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you told me you-- you had told me you were-- you were-- received a college scholarship at the age of 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Well, it was a joke in the house. My father used to come home and say, what grade are you in this week? Because I kept skipping grades. And they didn't know what to do with me. And his working with me and my brother almost kept us on grade level in terms of academically. And I developed a phenomenon, I guess, because I didn't have the written book that I developed a very good memory. So when I used to read something once, I would know it. So my reading comprehension was very well. And my memory was developed. Um.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you tell me a story. You had told me that, um, your family had come from Munich. And, um, your-- your mother-- your mother's mother had lived with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandmother lived in the same building just one floor below. And my-- Hitler-- there was an artist living in that building, a very well-known artist. I don't know the name. But Hitler was having his portrait painted. And he came with this entire entourage. And we were going down with the baby nurse, who was German, and my brother, who was a, you know, little kid. And he had seen that you click your heels. You know, that was the German style. [LAUGHS] And he clicked his heels and Hitler came. And he saw this blonde-- you saw the pictures-- blonde and blue-eyed child. And he picked me out of the stroller. And he said, this is the example of the Aryan race that we want to develop. [LAUGHS] Ironic.
